title: [Question] How can I centralise the blob info cache across multiple workers to enable ECR layer mounting?
body:
Hi folks,

I'm looking for advice on how to better leverage caching for the BlobMounting feature, specifically with AWS ECR, and would appreciate any insights.

**Context**

We replicate artifacts (OCI images) to AWS ECR using the podman/skopeo SDK. To benefit from the new [ECR cross-repository layer sharing feature](https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2026/01/amazon-ecr-cross-repository-layer-sharing/), we need BlobInfoCacheDir configured — otherwise the blob info cache lives only in memory.

**Why the in-memory cache hurts us**

No blob mounting. For each copy operation, Skopeo can't tell whether it has seen an artifact before, so it can't mount existing layers — copy times don't improve.
No cost reduction. Every operation is a fresh copy rather than a mount, so we don't get the associated savings.

**The core problem**

We run multiple worker pods that all perform replication, and each pod has its own local BlobInfoCacheDir. As a result:

The cache is not shared. A layer already replicated by one pod isn't known to the others, so shared/previously-replicated layers get re-copied unnecessarily.

**What I'm looking for**

I'd like to centralise the blob info cache (currently the SQLite DB under BlobInfoCacheDir) so all pods can share it. Has anyone tackled this? I'm considering options like a shared store (Redis) or a proper database, but I'm keen to hear recommended best practices before committing to an approach.

Any advice is greatly appreciated

Thanks in advance for your help,

Helio

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out.

I think the principal avenue I’d recommend is to move towards a more deterministic distribution design; the cache is not designed to provide _guaranteed_ matches (e.g. any software update can cause pre-existing entries to be ignored or deleted; we already had one complete change of the file format of the default database). E.g. if the same image should exist in multiple repos, explicitly copy it. (That, admittedly, runs into https://github.com/podman-container-tools/container-libs/issues/274, but that could be solved.)

In particular, I think it should be _generally unnecessary_ to rely on the non-exact matching (uncompressed vs. compressed layer, differently-compressed layers), apart from the very first push of a built image. That does still leave cross-repo mounts of exact duplicates.

---

As for sharing … the cache is designed to be concurrently accessed by multiple processes, so sharing it on a volume accessed from separate containers within a single kernel should probably work. Sharing it on a network filesystem accessed from multiple machines … probably not, IIRC the locks don’t propagate across NFS, and https://sqlite.org/lockingv3.html says the same thing.

I can _imagine_ a network-aware database client accessing the blob info cache for a truly cross-machine information sharing, but it sounds like way too much complexity and exposing internal details we’d like to keep hidden, for something that is supposed to be a low-cost opportunistic optimization opportunity. E.g. the useful BlobInfoCache API is already internal-only, and we plan fairly significant design changes to it in near future.

---

It’s quite possible that dedicated mirroring software like `oc-mirror` has designs that directly target and optimize blob reuse within destination registries, but I don’t know whether that’s actually the case.

Hello @mtrmac , 

First of all, thank you so much for your prompt response and assistance, I truly appreciate it!

When it comes to the sharing, it makes plenty of sense and I agree that sharing a common volume where different nodes will be writing to it may lead to a locking problem, such as an EFS system... we definitely don't want to do it.

I'm not familiar with `oc-mirror`, but definitely something I would be happy to look at.
____________

When it comes to `BlobInfoCache` we indeed noticed that's internal only, but one of our thoughts was that if that was exposed through SystemContext, we could perhaps consider injecting our own implementation of storage for those blobs, for example, in a ElastiCache? But at the same time, after doing some readings, we have noticed that's also not recommended to use, since it is _"deprecated"_.

______

Do you mind elaborating on what do you mean with _"I think the principal avenue I’d recommend is to move towards a more deterministic distribution design;"_? I agree 100% that the cache it is not meant to provide guarantee matches, but if we are able to maximise the effectiveness of this "cache", especially on distributed systems, like ours it would be great.

As far as I understand, the cache works as a _best-effort_, so if it doesn't exist or even if the file is corrupted, it _fallback_ to memory replication, which is absolutely fine.

______

If I could share, maybe what I'm trying to do is to maximise as much as I can the cache ratio to be able to benefit from Blob Mounting Feature of ECR where we can have cost reduction and more importantly swift distributions using "copy". At the same time, I'm also trying to reach out to ECR team on why they have designed that in a way that put a dependency on a client to make us benefit from this feature.

Thank you once again for your help,
Helio
